questions.question: What is the correct order of the agentic loop?
options: Plan → explore → commit → code
Explore → plan → code → commit
Code → explore → plan → commit
Explore → code → plan → commit
questions.explanation: The loop is explore → plan → code → commit. Reading the real code first corrects the agent's mental model before it plans, so the wrong target is caught while it's still cheap text.

questions.question: Per this phase, which four elements make a prompt specific?
options: The tone, the length, the formatting, and the politeness of the ask
The model name, the sampling temperature, the max-token budget, and a system prompt
The file, the constraint, the example to imitate, and the definition of done
A task title, a short summary, a time estimate, and a hard deadline
questions.explanation: Specificity is naming the file, the constraint, the example pattern to imitate, and a checkable definition of done — not raw length or model settings.

questions.question: What does 'feed context, don't describe it' mean?
options: Hand the agent the verbatim source (the file, screenshot, or log) rather than paraphrasing it
Write a longer, more careful prose description of the problem before the agent starts working
Always summarize the relevant files first so the agent reads fewer tokens off the raw source
Let the agent guess the missing details from memory to keep the working context window small
questions.explanation: Feeding means giving the source of truth verbatim — @file, an image, piped output — because your paraphrase is a lossy, confidently-incomplete summary.

questions.question: You need to bump a single constant in one config file from 3000 to 5000. Your agent offers plan mode. Best move?
options: Use plan mode anyway — planning every change builds a consistently safe habit
Skip the plan and just make the edit; the diff is obvious and one line
Use plan mode, then clear context, then implement in a fresh session
Route the change through a migration so it's reversible
questions.explanation: A one-line, obvious change is exactly when planning is pure ceremony. 'Always plan' overgeneralizes into waste; the others pile heavyweight process onto a trivial fix.

questions.question: An agent skips exploration, writes code that compiles and passes a quick eyeball, but at review turns out to solve the wrong problem. What does this reveal about the explore step?
options: Exploration mainly warms up the model before it starts editing, so its later output comes out more fluent
Without reading the real code, the agent invents the unseen parts plausibly, so wrong-target bugs hide
Exploration is only needed when the task spans several source files; a single-file change can skip it safely
Skipping the explore step makes the code fail to compile, an error that is caught long before it reaches review
questions.explanation: The danger is precisely that the code DOES compile — it solved the wrong problem. 'Warm up' is invented; single-file tasks also hide constraints; and the failure is not a compile error.

questions.question: What is the defining property of plan mode (vs. just chatting)?
options: It runs the agent on a cheaper, faster model so that planning costs you fewer tokens
It lets the agent read, reason, and propose a plan without writing anything to disk
It automatically commits the proposed plan to git as a checkpoint you can revert to
It hides the earlier conversation history so the working context window stays small
questions.explanation: Plan mode is read-only thinking: the agent proposes what it will do with no disk writes, so you correct cheap words instead of expensive code. It isn't a model swap, a commit, or a context reset.

questions.question: A button is misaligned only on mobile. Which prompt best follows 'feed, don't describe'?
options: The submit button in @src/components/SubmitBar.tsx looks off on small phone screens, kind of pushed over to the right side
Make the whole layout responsive and just fix any of the mobile issues you happen to notice across @src/components/
Here's a 375px screenshot [attached] and @src/components/SubmitBar.tsx — the button overflows on mobile; fix it, don't touch desktop
Something is clearly wrong with the CSS on phones for @src/components/SubmitBar.tsx; please go and review all of the stylesheets in there
questions.explanation: Only option C feeds verbatim sources — the actual screenshot and the actual component — plus a constraint. The others describe a layout the agent can't see or scope it so wide it must read your whole repo.

questions.question: Of these prompts for the SAME task, which is the WEAKEST by this phase's standards despite sounding thorough?
options: In cart.ts, dedupe items by SKU like mergeLines does; don't change totals; cart tests green
Make the cart code cleaner and more robust, and handle the duplicate-items situation well
Fix the duplicate line-items bug in @cart.ts; reuse mergeLines; done when npm test -- cart passes
Dedupe cart items by SKU in cart.ts, mirror mergeLines, keep totals identical, verify with the cart tests
questions.explanation: B names no file, no concrete constraint, no pattern to mirror, and no checkable done — 'cleaner' and 'robust' are wishes, not orders. A, C, and D each pin a file, a pattern, a constraint, and a verifiable finish line.

questions.question: You ask the agent to 'add caching to the API.' Its plan proposes an in-memory cache. Reading the plan, you realize you run several instances behind a load balancer. Why is catching this in the plan the textbook win?
options: Plans are billed at a lower per-token rate than the implementation code the agent goes on to write afterwards
A plan is the only artifact the agent is actually permitted to revise once it has already been written out for you
A plan-stage catch costs one sentence to fix; an implementation-stage catch costs a re-read, a re-write, and a polluted window
Once the code has actually been written, the agent can no longer read back its own earlier reasoning steps from the conversation
questions.explanation: The plan is the cheapest place to be wrong. The distractors are plausible but false: there is no separate plan billing tier, code is also revisable, and the agent can re-read its own work.

questions.question: Your prose description of a bug is careful and grammatically perfect. Why can feeding the verbatim source still beat it?
options: Pasted sources are stored outside the context window, so they cost the agent no tokens at all to read
Even careful prose is a lossy summary that omits details you didn't know mattered and can reflect stale memory of the code
Agents are simply unable to act on any information that is handed to them as plain natural-language prose alone
Screenshots and logs are always routed to a separate, much more accurate vision-only model that plain text prose can never reach
questions.explanation: Prose quality doesn't fix the two real problems: omission of unknowingly-important detail and staleness vs. the current file. The distractors invent token-free storage, a prose restriction, and a separate model.
